article_title: I run these 5 commands on every fresh Linux install to save hours of work
core_thesis: Setting up a fresh Linux system can be time-consuming, but by following five key steps—updating the system, selecting the fastest mirrors, bulk-installing packages, restoring dotfiles with chezmoi, and setting up system-wide backups—users can dramatically reduce setup time and avoid common pitfalls like dependency hell or data loss.
detailed_summary: The article presents a streamlined workflow for quickly configuring a new Linux installation. It begins by emphasizing the importance of running `sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade` (or equivalents for other distros) to sync the local package index and upgrade all packages, preventing dependency issues. Next, it recommends selecting the fastest mirrors using tools like MX Repo Manager or `nala` to speed up downloads. For installing applications, the author suggests using `apt-mark showmanual > packages.txt` on an old machine to export a list of manually installed packages, then transferring that file to the new system and running `sudo xargs apt install -y < packages.txt` to install everything at once. Alternatively, Tuxmate can generate a single install command. The fourth step covers backing up and restoring dotfiles using `chezmoi`, which pushes configuration files (e.g., .zshrc, kitty.conf) to a GitHub repo and applies them on a new machine with a single command. Finally, the article stresses setting up system-wide backups with tools like Timeshift to recover from breakage. The comments section includes discussions about the limitations of `apt-mark showmanual` (it lists all packages, not just manually installed ones) and the importance of off-site backups beyond Timeshift.

entities.name: Faisal Rasool
entities.role: Author of the article, a feature writer at How-To Geek with Linux expertise.

entities.name: How-To Geek
entities.role: Technology publication that published the article.

entities.name: chezmoi
entities.role: Tool for managing and restoring dotfiles from a GitHub repository.

entities.name: Timeshift
entities.role: System snapshot tool for creating and restoring backups on Linux.

entities.name: nala
entities.role: Command-line tool for selecting the fastest package mirrors.

entities.name: Tuxmate
entities.role: Online service that generates a single installation command for a selected set of packages.

key_data_points: Running `sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade` on a fresh install prevents dependency hell by syncing the local package index and updating all packages to their latest versions. (🟢 Relevant to your Homelab/DevOps: ensures a stable base for further installations and avoids time-consuming troubleshooting.)

Using `nala fetch` or a GUI mirror selector can dramatically speed up downloads by choosing the fastest mirror; slow mirrors can make updates excruciatingly slow. (🟢 Relevant to your Homelab: optimizes network usage in lab environments.)

The command `apt-mark showmanual > packages.txt` exports a list of all manually installed packages, which can be transferred to a new system and installed in bulk with `sudo xargs apt install -y < packages.txt`. (🟢 Relevant to your programming/DevOps: automates environment replication across machines.)

chezmoi allows you to back up dotfiles (e.g., .zshrc, kitty.conf) to a GitHub repo and restore them on a new system with `chezmoi init --apply <repo-url>`. (🟢 Relevant to your Red Teaming: ensures consistent tool configurations across attack machines.)

Timeshift creates system snapshots that can be reverted if an update breaks the system; the author lost data due to a broken update before adopting this practice. (🟢 Relevant to your Homelab: critical for disaster recovery and experimentation.)

Commenters noted that `apt-mark showmanual` lists all packages, not just manually installed ones, and suggested using `grep -i install ~/.bash_history` to filter. (🟢 Relevant to your programming: provides a more accurate method for package list generation.)
methodology_steps: Step 1: Run `sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade` (or `sudo pacman -Syu` on Arch, `sudo dnf upgrade` on Fedora) to sync the package index and upgrade all packages.
Step 2: Select the fastest mirrors using `sudo nala fetch` (if nala is installed) or a GUI tool like MX Repo Manager to speed up future downloads.

Step 3: On your old machine, run `apt-mark showmanual > packages.txt` to export a list of manually installed packages. Transfer the file to the new machine, then run `sudo xargs apt install -y < packages.txt` to install all packages in one go. Alternatively, use Tuxmate to generate a single install command.

Step 4: Install chezmoi with `sh -c "$(curl -fsLS get.chezmoi.io)" -- -b $HOME/.local/bin`. Create a blank GitHub repo for dotfiles. Initialize chezmoi with `chezmoi init`, add dotfiles (e.g., `chezmoi add ~/.zshrc`), commit and push to the repo. On a new machine, run `chezmoi init --apply <repo-url>` to restore configurations.

Step 5: Set up system-wide backups using Timeshift (pre-installed on many Debian-based distros) to create regular snapshots. For off-site backups, consider copying snapshots to external drives or using Clonezilla for full disk clones.
conclusions: The five-step approach (update, mirror selection, bulk package install, dotfile restoration, backups) significantly reduces the time and frustration of setting up a new Linux system. The author's personal experience with data loss underscores the necessity of backups.

Automating package installation and dotfile restoration via `apt-mark` and `chezmoi` enables rapid environment replication, which is valuable for users who frequently reinstall or manage multiple machines.

Commenters highlighted that `apt-mark showmanual` may include system packages, so filtering with bash history or manual review is recommended for accuracy. Additionally, backups should be stored off-site to protect against hardware failure.
personal_relevance_summary: This article directly addresses your Homelab and DevOps needs by providing a repeatable, automated workflow for Linux setup. As a programmer, you can leverage `apt-mark` and `chezmoi` to quickly replicate your development environment across machines, saving hours of manual configuration. The backup advice is crucial for your Red Teaming lab, where experimentation can break systems. The comment section also offers practical refinements (e.g., using bash history to filter packages) that improve the accuracy of the package list. Overall, this is a high-value read for anyone managing multiple Linux systems.
extra_advice: Consider creating a single bootstrap script that combines all five steps (update, mirror selection, package install from a curated list, chezmoi dotfile restore, and Timeshift setup). Store this script in a private GitHub repo and run it on every fresh install. For Red Teaming, also include tools like Metasploit, Nmap, and Burp Suite in your package list, and back up your `/etc/` configurations (e.g., for proxies or VPNs) using chezmoi.
